items.title: Booking action explicitness
items.description: Evaluate whether the lead clearly expresses reserve, hold, confirm stock, confirm package, confirm pickup, confirm dinner, confirm invoice, or confirm stay feasibility.
examples: High score example: Please hold one private-bath room for Saturday to Monday.
Low score example: Is your courtyard very photogenic.

items.title: Stay fact completeness
items.description: Evaluate whether the lead includes property or area, dates, nights, room count, adult and child or pet counts, room type, arrival timing, budget, and contact identity.
examples: High score example: property, dates, nights, room count, guest structure, and room preference are all present.
Low score example: We want to find a homestay to relax sometime.

items.title: Property specificity
items.description: Evaluate whether the inquiry is tied to a specific boutique homestay, old-town house, lake-side house, or mountain property.
examples: High score example: Friday at your Shuanglang sea-view house.
Low score example: Do you have anything suitable in Dali.

items.title: Guest structure and policy clarity
items.description: Evaluate whether the lead clearly states adult and child counts, child ages, pet status, elderly needs, extra bed requests, adjacent room requests, low-floor preference, child breakfast rules, or pet cleaning rules.
examples: High score example: 2 adults, 2 children ages 5 and 9, one small dog, and two adjacent rooms needed.
Low score example: It is a family trip and we will figure out the rest later.

items.title: Occasion and event scope clarity
items.description: Evaluate whether birthday, proposal, buyout, retreat, family gathering, wedding-eve, or travel-shoot scenarios include room count, nights, decor, dinner, photography, invoice needs, and contact ownership.
examples: High score example: anniversary for 2 nights around 3000 with decor and dinner.
Low score example: We just want a place that feels right for a proposal.

items.title: Package and add-on match
items.description: Evaluate whether the guest's interest in breakfast, dinner, pickup, private bath, tea by the fire, photography, craft sessions, or pet support broadly matches what the property can actually supply.
examples: High score example: room plus dinner plus pickup for a two-night stay.
Low score example: wants everything but does not know nights or room type.

items.title: Budget and resource feasibility
items.description: Evaluate whether the stated budget broadly matches peak dates, view rooms, private bath rooms, plunge pool rooms, buyout access, pet-friendly rooms, and adjacent-room needs.
examples: High score example: off-peak mountain-view room budget aligns with room type and dinner add-on.
Low score example: holiday buyout with photography and dinner at a very low budget.

items.title: Timing and arrival clarity
items.description: Evaluate whether urgency is backed by real travel facts such as tonight stay, train arrival, self-drive arrival time, pickup need, or return timing.
examples: High score example: We drive in at 9 p.m. tonight and can book immediately.
Low score example: Send the lowest price now or we are gone.

items.title: Decision ownership and reachability
items.description: Evaluate whether the person asking is the actual booker, family organizer, company admin, wedding planner, or shoot lead, versus someone casually asking for another person.
examples: High score example: I am the one booking and the dates and room count are fixed.
Low score example: I am just asking for a friend.

items.title: Risk and noise intensity
items.description: Absolute promises, over-occupancy, no registration, rule-breaking parties, pet-policy violations, refund complaints, influencer barter, shoot scouting, and pure channel cooperation should all increase risk_score and can override commercial temptation.
examples: High risk example: guarantee a specific room, let us party late, and skip full registration.
Low risk example: normal stay confirmation around dates, rooms, and package details.
